Crypto-backed loans carry risk of liquidation if collateral value drops. Rates and terms vary by platform. Not financial advice.

How to Use the Crypto Loan Calculator

Our free crypto loan calculator helps you plan crypto-backed loans by estimating interest costs, margin call thresholds, and liquidation prices. Whether you are borrowing against Bitcoin, Ethereum, or other collateral assets, this tool shows you the full cost and risk picture before you commit.

  1. Select your collateral — choose the cryptocurrency you will deposit as collateral and enter the amount.
  2. Set the LTV ratio — choose a Loan-to-Value percentage. Lower LTV means a smaller loan but a safer distance from liquidation.
  3. Enter the interest rate — input the annual percentage rate (APR) offered by your lending platform.
  4. Choose the loan period — select how long you plan to hold the loan.
  5. Review results — see your loan amount, total interest cost, monthly payments, margin call price, and liquidation price.

Key Features

  • Platform presets — quick-select configurations for Nexo, Aave, Binance, and other popular lending platforms.
  • Margin call calculator — see the exact collateral price that triggers a margin call for your specific LTV.
  • Liquidation price — know the price at which your collateral gets automatically sold.
  • Interest breakdown — see monthly and total interest costs over your chosen period.
  • Real-time prices — collateral values update with live CoinGecko data.

How Crypto-Backed Loans Work

Crypto-backed loans let you borrow stablecoins (USDT, USDC, DAI) or fiat currency against your crypto holdings without selling them. You deposit cryptocurrency as collateral and receive a loan based on the Loan-to-Value (LTV) ratio. No credit check is required because your collateral secures the loan.

The key advantage is that you maintain exposure to your crypto's upside potential while accessing liquidity. If Bitcoin is at $65,000 and you need $20,000, you can deposit 1 BTC as collateral (at 50% LTV) and borrow $32,500 without triggering a taxable event from selling.

Key Loan Concepts

TermDefinitionTypical Range
LTV (Loan-to-Value)Ratio of loan amount to collateral value25% — 75%
Margin CallThreshold where you must add collateral or repay75% — 85% LTV
LiquidationPlatform sells your collateral to repay loan83% — 90% LTV
APRAnnual interest rate on borrowed amount2% — 15%
Origination FeeOne-time fee charged when loan is created0% — 2%

LTV Ratio Explained

The Loan-to-Value ratio determines how much you can borrow relative to your collateral's value. It is the single most important factor in crypto loan safety.

LTV = Loan Amount / Collateral Value × 100%

A 50% LTV means your loan is half the value of your collateral. If your collateral is worth $10,000, you can borrow up to $5,000. The remaining $5,000 of collateral value acts as a safety buffer against price declines.

Lower LTV ratios are safer but give you access to less capital. Higher LTV ratios provide more liquidity but leave you closer to margin calls and liquidation if the collateral price drops.

Margin Call and Liquidation

When the price of your collateral drops, your LTV increases. If it rises above the margin call threshold (typically 75-85%), the platform notifies you to either add more collateral or repay part of the loan. If you fail to act and LTV exceeds the liquidation threshold (typically 83-90%), the platform automatically sells your collateral to repay the loan.

Margin Call Price = Entry Price × (Margin Call LTV / Initial LTV)

For example, if you deposit 1 BTC at $65,000 with 50% LTV and the margin call threshold is 75%: Margin Call Price = $65,000 × (50% / 75%) = $43,333. Bitcoin would need to drop 33% before you receive a margin call.

Comparing Crypto Lending Platforms

Different platforms offer different rates, LTV limits, and safety features. Here is a general comparison of the major categories:

Platform TypeAPR RangeMax LTVKey Feature
CeFi (Nexo, BlockFi)2% — 13%75%Simple UX, insurance on deposits
DeFi (Aave, Compound)1% — 10%80%Non-custodial, transparent rates
Exchange (Binance)3% — 15%65%Integrated with trading account
MakerDAO0.5% — 8%66%Borrow DAI directly, fully decentralized

CeFi platforms are the easiest to use and often offer the most competitive rates for larger loans. DeFi platforms provide transparency and non-custodial control but require technical knowledge (wallet management, gas fees). Exchange-based loans are convenient if you already have funds on the platform.

Safety Tips for Crypto Borrowing

  • Keep LTV below 50% — this gives you a significant buffer before margin calls. At 50% LTV with a 75% margin call threshold, your collateral can drop 33% before issues arise.
  • Set price alerts — configure notifications on your phone for the margin call price so you have time to react.
  • Keep reserve collateral ready — have additional crypto available to deposit quickly if the market drops.
  • Monitor during volatile periods — check your loan health factor during major market moves. Flash crashes can trigger liquidations faster than you can react.
  • Understand the liquidation process — some platforms liquidate the entire position at once, while others gradually liquidate only enough to bring LTV back below the threshold.
  • Consider using stablecoins as collateral — if you need a fiat loan, using stablecoins as collateral eliminates price volatility risk entirely.
  • Factor in all costs — beyond APR, account for origination fees, withdrawal fees, and any spread on the borrowed assets.

When Does a Crypto Loan Make Sense?

Crypto loans are most useful in these scenarios:

  • Avoiding taxable events — borrowing against crypto instead of selling avoids triggering capital gains taxes in most jurisdictions.
  • Maintaining long-term positions — if you are bullish on Bitcoin long-term but need short-term liquidity, a loan lets you access cash without selling.
  • Leveraging positions — borrow stablecoins, buy more crypto. This is essentially leverage and carries additional risk.
  • Bridging expenses — cover a temporary cash need (home repair, business expense) without disrupting your investment strategy.

Crypto loans are NOT recommended when you need funds for an extended period during a bear market, when you cannot afford to add collateral if prices drop, or when the interest rate exceeds the expected appreciation of your collateral.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is a safe LTV ratio for crypto loans?

Most experts recommend keeping LTV below 50%. This provides a 25-35% buffer before margin calls (typically triggered at 75-85% LTV). At 50% LTV, your collateral can lose approximately one-third of its value before you face a margin call, giving you time to react in volatile markets.

What happens if my crypto collateral drops in value?

As your collateral's price drops, your LTV increases. If it exceeds the margin call threshold, you will be asked to deposit more collateral or repay part of the loan. If you do not act and LTV exceeds the liquidation threshold, the platform automatically sells enough collateral to bring the loan back to a safe LTV level.

Can I repay a crypto loan early?

Most platforms allow early repayment without penalties. DeFi protocols like Aave and Compound have no fixed terms — you can repay at any time and only pay interest for the duration you held the loan. Some CeFi platforms may have minimum loan periods (typically 1-3 months).

Is the interest rate fixed or variable?

It depends on the platform. CeFi platforms like Nexo typically offer fixed rates for specific loan tiers. DeFi platforms like Aave use variable rates that change based on pool utilization — rates increase when more capital is borrowed and decrease when liquidity returns. Variable rates can fluctuate significantly during high-demand periods.

Do I still earn staking rewards on my collateral?

On most CeFi platforms, no — your collateral is locked and does not earn rewards. However, some DeFi protocols allow you to use liquid staking tokens (like stETH for Ethereum) as collateral, meaning you continue earning staking rewards while borrowing against your position.

What are the tax implications of crypto loans?

In most jurisdictions, borrowing against crypto is not a taxable event because you are not selling the asset. However, if your collateral is liquidated, that is treated as a sale and triggers capital gains or losses. Interest payments may be tax-deductible in some cases (particularly for business purposes). Consult a tax professional for your specific situation.
